title: The Fourth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## June 25, 2023

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Fourth Sunday after Pentecost falls in Ordinary Time, the longest liturgical season of the church year. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for June 25, 2023 would have included 1 Samuel 17:1a, 4-11, 19-23, 32-40 (David and Goliath); Psalm 9:9-20; 2 Corinthians 6:1-13 (Paul's plea for reconciliation); and Mark 4:35-41 (Jesus calming the storm). This sermon date occurred during the green-vestment season following Pentecost, a time focused on Christian living and spiritual growth rather than major liturgical festivals.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the midst of post-General Convention (June 2022) implementation, with ongoing discussions about LGBTQ+ inclusion policies that had been approved in Austin. The Diocese of Colorado, led by Bishop Kimberly Lucas (who took office in 2022), was continuing its transition period as a newer diocesan leader. No major Episcopal scandals or bishop elections were occurring nationally in late June 2023, making this a relatively stable period denominationally.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early summer heat in late June with temperatures in the high 80s to low 90s Fahrenheit. The city was preparing for the July 4th holiday week, with various community events scheduled. Saint John's Cathedral location in downtown Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood placed it near ongoing urban development and the regular summer activity of Colorado's capital city; no major local crises dominated the news cycle that particular week.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Hunter Biden trial was concluding in Wilmington, Delaware with closing arguments expected imminently (verdict came June 23). The Trump indictment trials were ongoing, with the January 6th classified documents case active and the New York civil fraud trial having concluded. The abortion debate remained heated following the June 2022 Supreme Court Dobbs decision, with ballot initiatives and state legislation continuing to dominate political discussions.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Russia-Ukraine war was entering its 16th month with grinding trench warfare and no major territorial changes. Turkey had just completed its election and NATO accession process, with broader Middle East tensions around Iran and Saudi Arabia relations. The global economic outlook was uncertain with banking sector concerns from earlier in spring 2023 still affecting confidence.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

American culture in late June 2023 was in summer entertainment mode, with the SAG-AFTRA and WGA strikes having begun in May and drastically altering the entertainment landscape. The zeitgeist reflected deep political polarization, with news cycles dominated by legal proceedings against prominent figures. A Denver congregation would have been aware of ongoing debates about religious institution roles in polarized society, particularly regarding LGBTQ+ inclusion and other cultural issues dividing American Christianity.
